question: [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / repay.onRepay] Can an unprivileged attacker acting as a repay callback receiver, holding a pre-signed EIP-712 Authorization from victim-lender authorizing attacker, call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onRepay (after debt is decremented but before transferFrom), then immediately call Midnight.withdraw(onBehalf=victim-lender) within the same callback, draining the lender's credit before the repay transferFrom executes? Preconditions: victim-lender has withdrawable credit; attacker holds a valid pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=victim-lender, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N, deadline=future); attacker is the repay callback address. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.repay(market, units, onBehalf=victim-lender, callback=attacker, data); inside onRepay, attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) granting themselves authorization over victim-lender; attacker then calls Midnight.withdraw(market, units, onBehalf=victim-lender, receiver=attacker); callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS; Midnight pulls tokens from attacker. Invariant tested: isAuthorized state must not be modifiable mid-repay in a way that enables unauthorized withdrawal of a third party's credit. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure of lender credit. Proof idea: fuzz (victim_credit, repay_units, pre_signed_auth) and assert victim-lender token balance is unchanged after the sequence.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / liquidate.onLiquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator, holding a pre-signed Authorization from borrower authorizing liquidator, call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onLiquidate (after collateral is transferred to receiver but before loan token transferFrom), then call Midnight.withdrawCollateral on behalf of borrower to seize remaining collateral beyond the liquidation amount? Preconditions: borrower is unhealthy with multi-collateral position; liquidator holds valid pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=borrower, authorized=liquidator, isAuthorized=true); liquidator is the liquidation callback. Call sequence: liquidator calls Midnight.liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, 0, borrower, false, receiver=liquidator, callback=liquidator, data); inside onLiquidate, liquidator calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized granting themselves authorization over borrower; liquidator calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, otherCollateralIndex, fullAmount, onBehalf=borrower, receiver=liquidator); callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS; Midnight pulls repaid units from liquidator. Invariant tested: collateral must not be withdrawable beyond liquidation rules during liquidation callback. Scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure beyond valid liquidation. Proof idea: assert borrower.collateral[otherCollateralIndex] == pre_liquidation_value after full sequence.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take.onSell] Can an unprivileged taker, acting as the seller callback in a sell-offer take, call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onSell to grant themselves authorization over the seller (borrower), then call Midnight.withdrawCollateral on behalf of the seller within the same callback while liquidationLocked is true, bypassing the health check that would otherwise block the withdrawal? Preconditions: seller has collateral and debt; taker holds pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=seller, authorized=taker, isAuthorized=true); taker is the takerCallback for a sell offer. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(sellOffer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, takerCallback=taker, data); inside onSell, taker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; taker calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf=seller, receiver=taker); callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS. Invariant tested: collateral withdrawal must pass isHealthy check regardless of liquidation lock state. Scoped impact: unauthorized collateral withdrawal from seller during take callback. Proof idea: assert isHealthy(market, id, seller) holds after withdrawCollateral or that withdrawCollateral reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take.onBuy] Can an unprivileged maker acting as the buyer callback in a buy-offer take call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onBuy to grant themselves authorization over the taker (seller), then call Midnight.withdrawCollateral on behalf of the taker within the same callback, draining the taker's collateral while the taker's debt has just been increased by the take? Preconditions: taker is the seller with newly increased debt; maker holds pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=taker, authorized=maker, isAuthorized=true); maker is the buyerCallback. Call sequence: taker calls Midnight.take(buyOffer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, takerCallback=0, data); inside onBuy (maker's callback), maker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; maker calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf=taker, receiver=maker); callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS. Invariant tested: taker's collateral must not be withdrawable by maker during take callback. Scoped impact: unauthorized collateral seizure from taker. Proof idea: fuzz (units, collateral, lltv) and assert taker collateral balance is unchanged.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / Midnight.multicall] Can an unprivileged attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically: (1) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized (granting attacker authorization over victim via pre-signed auth), (2) call Midnight.withdraw(onBehalf=victim) to drain victim's credit, (3) call Midnight.setIsAuthorized to revoke the authorization, leaving no trace? Preconditions: victim has withdrawable credit; attacker holds valid pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N); EcrecoverAuthorizer is authorized by victim in Midnight. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.multicall([EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth,sig), Midnight.withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker), Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, victim)]). Invariant tested: multicall must not allow authorization-grant + drain + revoke in a single atomic transaction. Scoped impact: direct loss of victim's credit. Proof idea: assert victim token balance unchanged after multicall sequence.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / isAuthorized delegation chain] Can an authorized delegate B (authorized by victim A via EcrecoverAuthorizer) use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to further authorize attacker C on behalf of A (since isAuthorized[A][B]=true satisfies the signer check), enabling C to drain A's position without A's direct consent? Preconditions: A has authorized B via EcrecoverAuthorizer (isAuthorized[A][B]=true); B signs an Authorization(authorizer=A, authorized=C, isAuthorized=true, nonce=currentNonce[A]); C is attacker-controlled. Call sequence: B calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(Authorization{authorizer=A, authorized=C, isAuthorized=true}, sig_by_B); Midnight.isAuthorized(A, B) returns true so signer check passes; C now has isAuthorized[A][C]=true; C calls Midnight.withdraw(onBehalf=A). Invariant tested: authorization delegation must not be transitive without explicit consent from the root authorizer. Scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal of A's funds by C. Proof idea: unit test asserting that B cannot grant C authorization over A via EcrecoverAuthorizer.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / repay.onRepay + setConsumed] Can an attacker acting as repay callback, after calling EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onRepay to gain authorization over the borrower, call Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, onBehalf=borrower) to cancel all of the borrower's active offers, causing a DoS on the borrower's market-making activity? Preconditions: borrower has active offers with consumed < maxUnits; attacker holds pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=borrower, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true); attacker is repay callback. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.repay(market, units, onBehalf=borrower, callback=attacker, data); inside onRepay, attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, borrower); callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS. Invariant tested: consumed state must not be modifiable by unauthorized parties during repay callback. Scoped impact: offer cancellation / fund freeze for borrower. Proof idea: assert consumed[borrower][group] == pre_repay_value after sequence.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / flashLoan.onFlashLoan] Can an attacker acting as flash loan callback call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onFlashLoan to grant themselves authorization over a victim, then call Midnight.withdraw(onBehalf=victim) within the same callback, repay the flash loan from the withdrawn funds, and net a profit? Preconditions: victim has withdrawable credit >= flash loan amount; attacker holds pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true); attacker is the flash loan callback. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.flashLoan(tokens=[loanToken], assets=[amount], callback=attacker, data); inside onFlashLoan, attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.withdraw(market, amount, victim, attacker); attacker approves Midnight for repayment; callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS. Invariant tested: flash loan callback must not enable unauthorized withdrawal of third-party credit. Scoped impact: direct loss of victim's credit. Proof idea: assert victim token balance unchanged after flash loan sequence.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take.onSell + isAuthorized[maker][ratifier]] Can an attacker acting as the seller callback in a take call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onSell to revoke the maker's ratifier authorization (isAuthorized[maker][ratifier]=false), causing subsequent takes of the same offer to revert with RatifierUnauthorized, effectively DoS-ing the maker's offers? Preconditions: maker has active offers with ratifier R; attacker holds pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=maker, authorized=R, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) signed by maker or an authorized delegate; attacker is the takerCallback. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker=attacker, receiver, takerCallback=attacker, data); inside onSell, attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(Authorization{authorizer=maker, authorized=R, isAuthorized=false}, sig); subsequent take of same offer reverts. Invariant tested: ratifier authorization must not be revocable by unauthorized parties during take callback. Scoped impact: DoS on maker's offers. Proof idea: assert isAuthorized[maker][ratifier] == true after take sequence.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / nonce front-running] Can an attacker front-run a victim's pending EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized transaction by submitting the same signed Authorization before the victim, consuming the victim's nonce N, causing the victim's transaction to revert with InvalidNonce, and leaving the victim's intended authorization state unset? Preconditions: victim has broadcast a signed Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N) off-chain; attacker observes the transaction in the mempool. Call sequence: attacker submits EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) with victim's signature before victim's tx; victim's tx reverts with InvalidNonce; victim's operator is not authorized. Invariant tested: a valid signed authorization must not be submittable by a front-runner to block the victim's intended state change. Scoped impact: fund freeze (victim cannot authorize their operator). Proof idea: assert that front-running the submission does not leave the victim in a worse state than if they had submitted themselves.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / nonce front-running + authorization flip] Can an attacker who observes a victim's signed Authorization(isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) in the mempool (intended to revoke an operator) front-run it with a different signed Authorization(isAuthorized=true, nonce=N) that the attacker previously obtained from the victim, consuming nonce N with isAuthorized=true, so the victim's revocation fails and the operator retains authorization? Preconditions: victim previously signed Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N) and shared it; victim later signs Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to revoke. Call sequence: attacker submits the isAuthorized=true authorization first; victim's isAuthorized=false tx reverts; operator retains authorization. Invariant tested: a victim's revocation intent must not be blockable by replaying an older same-nonce authorization. Scoped impact: unauthorized retention of operator access. Proof idea: assert isAuthorized[victim][operator] == false after victim's revocation attempt.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / EIP-712 domain separator with block.chainid] Can an attacker on a forked chain replay a victim's Authorization signature that was signed on the original chain, since EcrecoverAuthorizer computes the domain separator using block.chainid at call time (not at construction), and if the fork has the same chainid as the original chain at the time of signing, the signature is valid on both chains? Preconditions: a hard fork occurs that initially shares the same chainid; victim signed Authorization on original chain; attacker replays on fork. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) on forked chain with victim's original-chain signature; domain separator matches; authorization is set on fork. Invariant tested: signatures must not be replayable across chains with the same chainid. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization on forked chain enabling fund drain. Proof idea: differential test asserting domain separators differ after chainid divergence.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / ecrecover address(0) bypass] Can an attacker craft a degenerate signature (v, r, s) such that ecrecover returns address(0), and if authorization.authorizer == address(0), the check signer == authorization.authorizer passes, allowing the attacker to set isAuthorized for address(0) as authorizer? Preconditions: attacker constructs Authorization{authorizer=address(0), authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=future}; attacker crafts (v,r,s) such that ecrecover returns address(0). Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig); require(signer != address(0)) check catches this. Invariant tested: ecrecover returning address(0) must always revert. Scoped impact: gate bypass if address(0) authorization is exploitable. Proof idea: fuzz (v, r, s) and assert setIsAuthorized always reverts when ecrecover returns address(0).

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / self-authorization loop] Can an attacker who has been authorized by victim A call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves (attacker) as an authorized signer for A, then use that to sign further authorizations on behalf of A, creating an irrevocable authorization chain that persists even after A revokes the original authorization? Preconditions: A has authorized attacker (isAuthorized[A][attacker]=true); attacker signs Authorization(authorizer=A, authorized=attacker2, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N) using attacker's key; attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(Authorization{authorizer=A, authorized=attacker2, isAuthorized=true}, sig_by_attacker); A revokes attacker (isAuthorized[A][attacker]=false); attacker2 still has isAuthorized[A][attacker2]=true. Invariant tested: revoking a delegate must not leave residual authorizations granted by that delegate. Scoped impact: persistent unauthorized access to A's position. Proof idea: assert that after A revokes attacker, no attacker-derived authorization remains active.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / liquidate.onLiquidate + withdraw credit] Can an attacker acting as liquidation callback call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onLiquidate to grant themselves authorization over a lender, then call Midnight.withdraw(onBehalf=lender) to drain the lender's credit while the liquidation's withdrawable increase is still in-flight? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take.onBuy + take on behalf of maker] Can an attacker acting as the buyer callback in a buy-offer take call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onBuy to grant themselves authorization over the maker, then call Midnight.take(sellOffer, ..., taker=maker) on behalf of the maker, increasing the maker's debt without the maker's consent? Invariant tested: take on behalf of a user must require prior authorization, not authorization granted mid-callback. Scoped impact: unauthorized debt increase for maker. [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / repay.onRepay + withdrawCollateral] Can an attacker acting as repay callback call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onRepay to grant themselves authorization over the borrower, then call Midnight.withdrawCollateral(onBehalf=borrower) to drain the borrower's collateral while the borrower's debt has just been reduced (making the position healthy enough to pass the health check)? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / multicall + take authorization] Can an attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically: (1) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to grant attacker authorization over victim-taker, (2) call Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker=victim, receiver=attacker, takerCallback=0, data) on behalf of victim, increasing victim's debt without victim's consent? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / EcrecoverAuthorizer authorized by victim + callback reentrancy into setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is the repay callback call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onRepay, where EcrecoverAuthorizer itself calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized, which is a state-changing call on Midnight during an active Midnight execution (repay), and verify whether this nested Midnight.setIsAuthorized call corrupts any in-flight state (e.g., position storage pointers, market state)? [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / deadline == block.timestamp boundary] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with deadline == block.timestamp, satisfying the require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) check, and use this to grant authorization in the same block as the deadline, potentially enabling a same-block attack sequence where the authorization is used and then expires? Preconditions: attacker holds pre-signed Authorization(deadline=block.timestamp); attacker submits in the same block. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) with deadline == block.timestamp; authorization is set; attacker immediately calls Midnight.withdraw(onBehalf=victim). Invariant tested: deadline boundary must not allow same-block authorization-and-drain attacks. Scoped impact: direct loss of victim's credit. Proof idea: fuzz (deadline, block.timestamp) and assert authorization cannot be used after deadline.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / EIP-712 struct hash with memory Authorization] Can an attacker exploit the fact that Authorization is passed as memory (not calldata) to EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, and craft a transaction where the ABI-encoded Authorization struct contains extra padding or non-canonical encoding that causes keccak256(abi.encode(AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH, authorization)) to produce a different hash than expected, potentially matching a hash the victim signed for a different purpose? Preconditions: attacker constructs a non-canonical ABI encoding of Authorization; victim signed a canonical encoding. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorizer with non-canonical Authorization memory struct. Invariant tested: EIP-712 struct hash must be canonical and collision-resistant. Scoped impact: signature forgery / gate bypass. Proof idea: differential fuzz test asserting hashStruct(canonical) != hashStruct(non_canonical) for all valid Authorization values.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / repay.onRepay + repay reentrancy] Can an attacker acting as repay callback call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized inside onRepay to grant themselves authorization over the borrower, then call Midnight.repay(market, units2, onBehalf=borrower, callback=0, data) again within the same callback, reducing the borrower's debt a second time without paying for the first repay yet, effectively getting a free debt reduction? Preconditions: borrower has debt >= units + units2; attacker holds pre-signed Authorization(authorizer=borrower, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true); attacker is repay callback. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.repay(market, units, borrower, callback=attacker, data); inside onRepay, attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.repay(market, units2, borrower, callback=0, data2) (nested repay, attacker pays units2); outer repay then pulls units from attacker. Invariant tested: debt reduction must correspond to exactly one token payment per repay call. Scoped impact: accounting corruption / bad debt. Proof idea: assert borrower.debt == initial_debt - units - units2 and total tokens pulled == units + units2.
